title: Eisenstein Congruences and Euler Systems
speaker: Eric Urban
affiliation: Columbia University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: GALOIS REPRESENTATIONS AND AUTOMORPHIC FORMS SEMINAR

proof_stages.label: Cyclotomic Euler-system target and Eisenstein-congruence motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from cyclotomic units and their norm relations, recalls horizontal Eisenstein congruences as a source of Galois cohomology classes, and formulates the guiding problem of recovering the cyclotomic classes integrally by an automorphic construction.

proof_stages.label: Vertical finite-slope deformation through a critical Eisenstein point
proof_stages.summary: After contrasting ordinary Hida families with the finite-slope Coleman theory, the speaker applies Coleman’s construction to the critical Eisenstein series whose slope is exactly its critical value, producing a family through the Eisenstein point.

proof_stages.label: Rational Galois cohomology classes from the reducible specialization
proof_stages.summary: The Coleman family gives an irreducible family of two-dimensional Galois representations that becomes reducible at the critical Eisenstein point, and a suitable lattice yields a cohomology class which, at weight two and character chi, should match the chi-component of the cyclotomic unit class.

proof_stages.label: Integral overconvergent Hecke-algebra framework
proof_stages.summary: To make the preceding construction integral, the speaker fixes level and weight, introduces finite-slope overconvergent modular-form spaces, their Hecke algebras, trace maps, Coleman neighborhoods, the completed local weight algebra, and the Eisenstein specialization map.

proof_stages.label: Pseudo-representations and the reducibility ideal
proof_stages.summary: Since an honest Galois representation over the whole Hecke algebra is unavailable, a Wiles-style pseudo-representation is used; its Eisenstein specialization is reducible, the associated off-diagonal term generates the reducibility ideal, and modulo the square of that ideal it gives cohomological classes, though not yet a compatible family.

proof_stages.label: Annihilator and differential construction of compatible tensor classes
proof_stages.summary: An exact sequence involving Kähler differentials and an annihilator eta of the relevant cotangent module are used to multiply the off-diagonal pseudo-representation class into the integral image, producing submodules D_n of tensor-product cohomology classes, with an equivalent componentwise description and an unconditional version at infinite p-level.

proof_stages.label: Corestriction relation and Euler factors
proof_stages.summary: The behavior under corestriction is reduced to the image of the annihilator under the natural map between group algebras, and the claimed formula gives the expected Euler factors, so the D_n satisfy the norm-compatibility pattern of an Euler system in tensor-product form.

proof_stages.label: Weight-independence and final refinements
proof_stages.summary: At infinite p-power level the resulting modules are asserted to be independent of the starting critical weight via twisting isomorphisms and weight-independent Hecke algebras, and the speaker records easier variants using the critical Eisenstein ideal and possible cuspidal analogues.

examples.name: Ordinary Hida family of Eisenstein series
examples.note: The ordinary Eisenstein family is used as a familiar comparison for vertical congruences before moving to the non-ordinary Coleman situation; it illustrates the concept but is not the construction being proved.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker invokes Hida’s ordinary family starting from an ordinary Eisenstein series and states that it gives ordinary Eisenstein series, but does not compute or trace the family in this case.

passes.reason: An Eisenstein Hida family is a standard infinite p-adic family but is non-cuspidal/reducible compared with generic cuspidal families, and it is used as a motivating construction rather than to refute a claim.
The Eisenstein Hida family is noncuspidal/reducible and ordinary of minimal slope, and it is used as a motivating construction rather than to refute a claim.
It is a standard Eisenstein/non-cuspidal Hida component with reducible Galois representations, and ordinary means minimal p-slope 0; it is used constructively, not to refute a claim.
an Eisenstein Hida family lies on the reducible/noncuspidal Eisenstein locus, with ordinary slope zero at the boundary of the slope spectrum, and is used constructively rather than as a refutation.

A Hida family of Eisenstein series is a systematic ordinary eigenfamily but is reducible/non-cuspidal at the Galois-representation level, used here as the motivating construction rather than to refute a claim.
It is a noncuspidal reducible Eisenstein Hida family, with ordinary slope zero, used as background rather than to refute a claim.
The ordinary Eisenstein Hida family is a slope-zero boundary/noncuspidal reducible eigenfamily used as a constructive model, not to refute a claim.
An Eisenstein Hida component is noncuspidal/reducible boundary data, and ordinary means minimal U_p-slope, while it is used constructively rather than to refute a claim.

The ordinary Eisenstein Hida family is used as the standard vertical-congruence source rather than to refute a claim, and its Eisenstein/reducible non-cuspidal nature is structurally less generic than a cuspidal family.
Used as the basic vertical-congruence construction, and structurally it is a noncuspidal/reducible Eisenstein Hida family with ordinary slope zero, the minimal slope case.
the ordinary Eisenstein Hida component is a slope-zero boundary case with reducible/noncuspidal Eisenstein structure, used constructively rather than adversarially
an Eisenstein Hida family is a reducible/noncuspidal boundary case, and ordinary means minimal U_p-slope rather than an obstruction to a claim

examples.name: CM-form replacement giving anticyclotomic Euler systems
examples.note: The final remark says that replacing Eisenstein series by CM cusp forms yields anticyclotomic Euler systems, serving as an optional indication of how the strategy extends in a cuspidal setting.
examples.engagement_reason: The CM-form replacement is only noted at the end as an analogous strategy yielding anticyclotomic Euler systems, with no construction or verification carried out.

passes.reason: CM forms are a systematic special class with extra complex-multiplication/dihedral symmetry, here used as a constructive replacement to obtain anticyclotomic Euler systems, not as a counterexample.
CM modular forms have extra endomorphism/dihedral symmetry in a systematic family, and here serve as a replacement source for anticyclotomic Euler systems rather than as counterexamples.
A CM modular form is identifiable as a systematic extra-symmetry/dihedral form used as a replacement to construct anticyclotomic Euler systems, not as a counterexample.
CM modular forms have systematic extra dihedral/complex-multiplication symmetry and are used as a replacement construction for anticyclotomic Euler systems, not to disprove a claim.
CM forms form systematic infinite families with extra CM/dihedral symmetry, used as a substitute to obtain anticyclotomic Euler systems rather than as a counterexample.
CM forms have extra dihedral/complex-multiplication symmetry and occur in infinite families, and here they are used constructively to build anticyclotomic Euler systems.
CM modular forms form systematic dihedral families with extra self-twist/complex-multiplication symmetry and are used constructively to obtain anticyclotomic Euler systems.
CM forms are an infinite special cuspidal family with extra dihedral/complex-multiplication symmetry, used as a constructive replacement producing anticyclotomic Euler systems.

The CM-form replacement is presented as a constructive workaround producing anticyclotomic Euler systems, and CM forms are structurally special through their extra complex-multiplication/dihedral symmetry.
Used as an alternative positive construction of anticyclotomic Euler systems, with CM forms having extra complex-multiplication symmetry but belonging to systematic infinite families.
CM modular forms have extra complex-multiplication/dihedral symmetry and are used as a constructive replacement to produce anticyclotomic Euler systems
CM modular forms are used as a successful replacement yielding anticyclotomic Euler systems and are structurally special through their extra complex-multiplication symmetries
